hints.Aquilifer: 'Aquilifer' is a noun — the subject of the sentence
hints.hostes: 'hostes' is a noun — the object of the preposition

hints.Present: Present of 'irruere' would be 'irruit' (same spelling), but in historical narrative Caesar uses the perfect — the doubled -i- signals perfect

hints.ruo, ruere, rui, rutum: These are the principal parts of 'ruere' (to rush/fall) — the base verb without the ir- prefix
hints.rumpo, rumpere, rupi, ruptum: These are the principal parts of 'rumpere' (to break)
hints.curro, currere, cucurri, cursum: These are the principal parts of 'currere' (to run)

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'aquilifer' with genitive 'aquiliferi' is 2nd declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'aquilifer' has genitive 'aquiliferi' like 'puer, pueri'
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -us — 'aquilifer' is 2nd declension like 'puer'

hints.Ablative: 'in' + ablative means location — here 'in hostes' means 'into the enemy' (motion toward), so accusative

hints.Nominative: 'hostes' could be nominative plural, but governed by 'in' with motion, it's accusative
hints.Ablative: Ablative plural would be 'hostibus' — 'hostes' is accusative plural here

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'hostis' with genitive 'hostis' is 3rd declension (i-stem)
hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'hostis' is 3rd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns end in -us with genitive -us — 'hostis' has genitive 'hostis' (3rd i-stem)

translation: The eagle-bearer charged into the enemy
